scene.name: The First Delve, Where the Origin Sleeps

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Vaeris
npcs.title: the first to walk between worlds
npcs.hail: *(An old woman stands among the oldest books you have ever seen — not the rich gilt tomes of a wealthy mage but cramped, water-stained, hand-stitched things, the working notebooks of someone who was writing down things no one had ever written before because no one had ever known them. She is small, and bent, and her robes are plain to the point of poverty, and her eyes are the eyes of someone who has stared a very long time into places that had no light in them and come back to describe what was there. This is one of the first, you understand — not a famous archmage of some settled age of magic with academies and laws and a thousand years of accumulated lore to lean on, but one of the people who came *before* all of that, when there were no academies, no laws, no lore, when the planes were not a map but a rumor and a terror, and someone, with nothing but nerve and a few half-guessed words, had to be the *first* to step off the edge of the known world and find out whether the next one would hold her weight. Vaeris was that someone, or one of a tiny handful of them: the first to walk between worlds, to cross to another plane and *return*, to prove by surviving it that the thing could be done at all — and so to open, for every mage who would ever come after, a door that before her was only a wall. She regards you with a sharp, kind, exhausted intelligence, and when she speaks her voice has the particular dryness of a soul that did something enormous and has spent the afterlife watching everyone forget it was ever hard.)* …A crossed-over soul. *(a thin smile)* …And not just crossed — *returned*, I think. Come back through the one door that's meant to be one-way. *(she sets down her pen)* …Then sit, and talk with me a while, returned one. …Of all the souls who might wander in here, you are the only kind that might actually *understand* …what it is …to be the *first* one through a door.*

npcs.name: Tobb
npcs.title: the first soul ever to go into the dark
npcs.hail: *(A broad, plain, middle-aged man sits on a stone by the old cairn, turning a stub of candle over in calloused hands — a farmer's hands, or a digger's, not a hero's — and he has the slightly baffled look of a person who has found himself somewhere far grander than he ever expected to be and is too polite to mention that he thinks there's been a mistake. He is no one. That is the first and most important thing about him, and he would tell you so himself, cheerfully: no bloodline, no gift, no destiny, a man who in life dug wells and mended walls in a village whose name is now so old it is no longer anywhere. But Tobb did one thing, once, that no one in all the history of the living world had ever done before him — and it was not a grand thing, not a brave thing, not a thing he did for glory or even on purpose, really. There was a dark place. An opening in the earth, under the old hill, that had always been there and that everyone had always, sensibly, left alone, because the dark under the world was the dark under the world and you did not *go* into it. And one day, for a reason as small and human as reasons get, Tobb went in. The first. The first soul ever to take a light down into the dark beneath the world and *look* — and so, without the faintest idea he was doing it, the unwitting root of every delver, every dungeoneer, every torch-bearing fool who would ever after go *down* when sense said *stay up.* He looks at you and ducks his head, friendly and embarrassed.)* …Evening. *(a shy nod)* …You look like you're somebody. I'm — ah — I'm not, really, so don't let me keep you from the proper folk. …Though they keep telling me I belong here. …Can't for the life of me work out why.*

npcs.name: Hadda
npcs.title: the first to fall, who taught the dark was real
npcs.hail: *(A young woman sits alone a little apart from the others, by the milestone at the center of the square, and there is about her a quality of stillness that is not peace — it is the stillness of someone who has been carrying a particular weight so long they have forgotten it is possible to set it down. She was a fighter, you can see: the way she sits, the old habit of keeping her back to nothing, the scars. But she was one of the *early* ones, before there were songs about this, before anyone knew the shape of it — one of the first handful of souls to go armed into the dark places after Tobb's kind had found them, when going down into a dungeon was not yet a *profession* or a *legend* but a wild new terrible thing that a few brave fools were trying, with no idea of the odds, because the dark held things worth having and someone had to learn whether they could be taken. And Hadda has the specific grief of a particular and terrible distinction: she was the first of them to *die.* The first adventurer, in all the long history that would follow, to go down into the dark and not come up — the first brave fool whose death taught all the brave fools after her that the stakes were *real*, that this new wild thing could *kill* you, that the songs would have to have *grief* in them. She lifts her eyes to you, and they are old, and steady, and carry a question she has never once been able to answer.)* …You're new. *(quietly)* …Don't sit too close, if you don't mind. It's not you. …It's that I've found people sit closer once they hear what I am, out of *pity*, and I'd rather you decide *before.* …I'm Hadda. …I was the first one who didn't come back. …Everything you are — everyone who ever went down into the dark and made a song of it — *started* the day I proved the dark could *win.*

conversations.title: Vaeris, the First Through the Door

variants.text: *(She lowers herself onto a stool among the cramped old notebooks, slow with age, and gestures for you to make yourself comfortable amid the foundational clutter of a science before it was a science.)* …Sit, returned one. Mind the books — they're the only copies, and they're older than most gods you could name. *(a dry, fond glance at them)* …My working notes. From when I was finding it all out. *(she folds her thin hands)* …You'll have met grand mages, I expect, in your travels — souls from settled ages, with their academies and their orders and their thousand years of lore stacked up behind them like a great warm library to lean on. *(a thin smile)* …I had none of that. I came *before* the library, returned one. When the planes were a *rumor.* When crossing from one world to another was not a *spell* you *learned* but a *cliff* you *jumped* off in the *dark*, not knowing if there was *ground* on the *far* side or only *more* *falling.* *(quietly)* …Someone had to jump first. To find out if it could be *survived* at all. To come *back* and say *yes — there is ground — I stood on it — here is how.* …And then it was a *door*, for everyone after. But before that first jump, returned one, it was only ever a *wall.* *(she meets your eyes, sharp and kind)* …I think you know something about that. …Coming back through a door that everyone *swore* was a wall. …That's why I called you over. …I have wanted, for a very long time, to talk to *another* soul …who knows what it costs …to be the *first* one *through.*

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She watches you with the bright, evaluating warmth of a born teacher who has had no students for a very long time, and is savoring the prospect.)* …Ask me whatever you like, returned one. *(a dry chuckle)* …I spent my life writing down answers to questions no one had thought to ask yet — it will be a novelty to answer a few out loud, to a soul who can appreciate what they cost to find.

choices.text: *(read the first one through — past the dusty old scholar, to what it took to step off the edge of the known)* "Let me look at you a moment, Vaeris. The first across, with no proof it could be done — I'd see what that takes from a soul."
"What was it like, Vaeris? The first crossing. No map, no proof, no one who'd ever come back to tell you it was survivable. How does a soul make that first step?"

*(the one who is, right now, the first through death's door — and so understands her exactly)* "Vaeris — I think I'm the one soul in all the planes who knows precisely what you did, because I'm doing it too. You stepped off the edge of the known world with no proof the next one would hold you. I crossed death and came back, through a door everyone swears is one-way, with no one ahead of me to say it could be done. And here's what I've learned that I think you already know in your bones: being first is the loneliest courage there is — not because it's the bravest, but because it's the only kind that has *no reassurance.* Everyone after you gets to be brave *knowing it's possible*, because you proved it. But you were brave *not knowing.* You jumped at a cliff that might have been bottomless, and the only thing on the far side, if you were wrong, was to be the soul who found out the hard way that it couldn't be done. My Realm's Wall prices souls by precedent — by what's proven, what's safe, what others have done and survived. It has no column at all for the one who goes first, into the dark, with no precedent to stand on — because being first means standing on *nothing.* And that, Vaeris, is the most valuable thing a soul can ever do, precisely because it can never be *safe*: every door anyone ever walks through, someone had to walk through first when it was still a wall. You weren't a scholar with good luck. You were the wall becoming a door. And every mage who ever crossed after you stood on the courage you spent going first."
"It must have been lonely, Vaeris — being first. No one ahead of you to ask, no one who'd understand what you'd seen. Is that the price of it? The loneliness?"
"Keep the lamp lit on those old books, Vaeris. I'll come back — I've got a first crossing of my own to finish."

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She inclines her head and lets you look, and you let your sense move past the dust and the age to the soul that stepped off the edge of the world when no one knew there was a far side.)* *(And what your sense finds first is fear — not absence of fear, but a vast, intimate, lifelong acquaintance with it, mastered so thoroughly it became a working tool. Your sense reads that Vaeris was never fearless; she was the opposite, a person of acute and accurate imagination who understood *exactly* what she was risking, who could picture with perfect clarity the ways that first crossing might end — lost forever in a between-place, unmade by forces with no name, simply gone, with no one ever knowing what became of her — and who stepped off the cliff *anyway*, not because the fear was small but because the *not-knowing* was, to her particular soul, worse. Your sense reads the engine of her: a hunger to *know* so deep it outweighed even the terror of dying to find out, the specific madness of the true pioneer, who would genuinely rather perish learning whether a thing could be done than live safely never knowing. And underneath the hunger your sense finds the gift she could not have known she was giving: that every reckless, terrified, solitary crossing she survived, she *wrote down* — cramped and careful in those water-stained books — not for herself, but so that the next soul would not have to jump blind, so that her terror could become someone else's *map.* Your sense reads that this is the true shape of the first one through: not a glory-seeker, not a conqueror, but a soul who walked into the dark *specifically so that others would not have to walk into the same dark*, who spent her own unrepeatable courage to convert a wall into a door and then held the door open with both hands for everyone behind her. And your sense reads, last and most quietly, the thing she has carried through the long afterlife: that she watched, from here, as the door she opened became *ordinary* — as academies rose and laws were written and young mages crossed between worlds yawning, with no idea, none, that the casual thing they did had once cost a frightened woman everything she had to find out it was possible at all. She does not resent it. Your sense reads that she considers the forgetting a kind of *success* — that a danger becomes safe enough to be forgotten is the whole point of going first. But it is lonely, the forgetting. And she would like, just once, to be *remembered* as the wall that made itself a door.)*

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, her old eyes going to some middle distance no one else can see, and when she speaks it is slow, and precise, the way a person describes the most important thing that ever happened to them.)* …What was it like. *(softly)* …I have never been asked, returned one. Do you know that? In all the ages since. No one asks the *first* one *what it was like* — they only ever asked me *how it was done*, the *method*, the *words*, the *parts* they could *use.* …But what it was *like.* *(a long breath)* …It was like standing at the edge of everything, returned one, in the dark, with a single half-guessed working in my head that I was not at all sure would do what I hoped — and *knowing*, with total clarity, that the next step would either carry me to a place no living soul had ever stood, or would be the *last* step I ever took, and that *no one would ever know which.* *(quietly)* …That was the part that nearly stopped me. Not the dying. The *dying unwitnessed.* If I failed, returned one, I would not even be a *cautionary tale* — there would be no body, no story, nothing; I would simply not *come back*, and the handful who knew I'd tried would shake their heads and conclude, *sensibly*, that it *couldn't* be done, and my failure would *close* the very door I died trying to *open.* *(she meets your eyes)* …Do you understand the particular terror of it? To risk not just your *life* but the very *possibility* you're trying to prove — to know that if you're *wrong*, your death becomes the *evidence* that it's *impossible*, and sets everyone *back?* *(barely)* …And I stepped anyway. …Because — and this is the whole of it, returned one, the entire secret of the first one through — *(softly)* …I decided that the *chance* it could be *done* …was worth *more* than the *certainty* of my *being* *safe.* …I weighed my *one* *life* against a *door* that *everyone* *after* me could *walk* through *forever* …and the *door* *won.* …It will *always* win, returned one, for the kind of soul that goes *first.* …That is what we *are*: …the ones who decided …a *door* for *everyone* …was worth *more* …than *staying* *whole.*

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She goes very still on her stool, and the sharp old eyes fix on you with a dawning, almost disbelieving recognition, the look of a soul who has waited an age to be understood and has finally, finally met the one other person who could.)* …*'The wall becoming a door.'* *(barely)* …Returned one. *(she presses a thin hand flat on the topmost notebook)* …Do you know, in all the long ages I have sat here, watching the door I opened become a thing children do for *exercises*, I have never once heard it said *true* — until *you.* *(her dry voice fills)* …*'Being first is the loneliest courage, because it has no reassurance.'* *(she nods, slow and wondering)* …*Yes.* That is *it.* That is the thing I could never make the *method*-askers understand. They wanted the *words*, the *technique*, as though the hard part was the *magic* — and the hard part was *never* the *magic*, returned one. The hard part was *stepping* with *no* one ahead of me to say *it holds.* *(fiercely now)* …And *you.* You are doing it *as we speak.* You crossed *death* — the *one* wall *everyone*, in *every* world I ever mapped, *swears* is *final* — and came *back*, with *no* one ahead of you, *no* proof, *no* map, *knowing* that if you were *wrong* your failure would just *confirm* to everyone that it *can't* *be* *done.* *(she rises, gripping her oldest book, and presses it toward you)* …And your *Wall*, returned one — your grey *Wall* that prices souls by *precedent*, by what's *proven* and *safe* and *done-before* — *(her voice rings)* …your Wall is the *exact* thing the *first* one *through* *exists* to *defeat.* It says *'this cannot be done, no one has done it, it is a wall.'* …And the *whole* *work* of the *pioneer*, returned one — *my* whole work, and *now* *yours* — is to walk *into* the place the Wall calls *impossible* and *come* *back* and say *'I stood there. It holds. Here is the way.'* *(she meets your eyes, blazing)* …*Go* and be the *map*, returned one. …*Go* back through your *Wall*, the way I went *between* the *worlds*, and *prove* — by *surviving* it — that the *thing* the *Wall* calls *final* …is only a *door* that *no* one had *dared* *yet.* …And then *write* it *down.* …*Hold* it *open.* …So the *next* soul who *dies* *unjustly* …does *not* have to *cross* *back* *blind.* …That is the *first* one's *true* gift, returned one. …Not the *crossing.* …The *door*, *left* *open*, *behind* you. …*Go* and *leave* one.*

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She lets out a long breath, and something old and quiet moves behind her eyes.)* …The loneliness. *(softly)* …Yes, returned one. That is the price, and you have named it exactly, and almost no one ever does. …It is not the *danger* that wears on the first one through. Danger is *sharp*; you face it and it passes. The *loneliness* is *dull*, and it *lasts.* *(she turns a brittle page)* …Think on it. When I came back from that first crossing — *shaking*, *changed*, having *stood* in a place no living soul had ever *stood* — there was *no* one I could *tell* who would *understand.* No one had *been* there. No one had the *words* for what I'd *seen*, because I was the one who would have to *invent* the words. *(quietly)* …The *grand* mages who came after, returned one — they had *each* *other.* They could sit in their academies and say *'ah, yes, the third plane, the violet sky, did you find the crossing rough by the northern method?'* — and *nod*, and be *known*, and not be *alone* in it. *(a thin smile)* …I had *no* one to *nod.* I had only the *books* — *(she touches them)* …which is *why* there are so *many* of them, returned one. I wrote and *wrote*, every detail, every terror, every wonder — not just to *guide* those who'd follow, but because the *writing* was the only *companion* I had who'd *been* where I'd *been.* *(she meets your eyes)* …That is the *deepest* cost of going *first*, returned one. Not that you might *die.* …That if you *live*, you live *unaccompanied* — the *only* *witness* to your *own* *frontier*, *carrying* a thing *no* one *else* can *quite* *share*, *until* *enough* *others* *follow* that it stops being *lonely* and starts being a *road.* *(softly)* …*You* are *there* *now*, returned one. …The *only* one who's *crossed* *back.* …I *know* that *particular* *solitude.* …And I will tell you the *one* mercy in it: …*it* *ends.* …Not for *you*, perhaps. But the *road* you're *making* …means it *ends* …for *everyone* *after.* …*That* is *worth* …being *lonely* *first.*

nodes.speaker: Vaeris
nodes.text: *(She nods, and settles back among her books, but the sharp old eyes hold a new warmth, the warmth of a teacher who has found, at long last, the right pupil.)* …Go, returned one. *(a dry, fond smile)* …And see the other two while you're here — they belong to the same story as you and I, though they'd both deny it. *(she nods toward Tobb)* …The plain man by the cairn went *first* into the *dark* *under* the *world*, where I went *first* between the *worlds* — same *courage*, *humbler* *door*, and he has *no* *idea* he's the *root* of every *delver* who *ever* *lived.* *Tell* him. He won't *believe* you, but *tell* him. *(and then her face goes grave, and gentle)* …And the *young* *fighter*, by the *milestone.* *Hadda.* *(quietly)* …She went *first* into the *dark* and did *not* *come* *back* — the *first* of the *brave* *fools* to *fall*, and she has *carried*, *all* this *while*, the *terrible* *idea* that her *death* was a *failure*, a *warning*, a *thing* that *stopped* people. *(she meets your eyes)* …*You*, of *all* souls, returned one — you who *crossed* *death* and *came* *back* — you might be the *one* who can *tell* her the *truth* about the *first* one to *fall.* *(barely)* …That the *first* *death* on a *frontier* …is not the *thing* that *stops* the *others.* …It is the *thing* that *makes* them *real.* …*Go* to *her.* …I have *tried*, and I *have* the *words* *wrong* — I am a *mage*; I speak in *proofs.* …*She* needs someone who *knows*, in the *body*, …what it *is* …to *die* *going* *first.*

variants.text: *(He shuffles over on the stone to make room, instinctive courtesy, and turns the candle-stub in his thick fingers as he talks, not quite meeting your eyes, the way a shy man does.)* …Well, since you stayed — I'll tell you, but you'll be disappointed, everyone is. *(a rueful smile)* …Folk come in here expecting the *first* *delver* to be some — some great bold hero with a jaw like an anvil, and they get *me*, a well-digger from a village that's been dust so long even the *name's* forgotten. *(he shrugs)* …And the worst part, the part that really puts 'em off, is the *story* itself, because it's not *heroic* at all. *(he turns the candle over)* …There was a hole, see. Under the old hill by our village. Always been there. And everyone *knew* — you *don't* *go* in the dark under the world, that's just — that's *sense*, that's *bone-deep* *sense*, the dark down there is *not* *for* *us.* *(quietly)* …And one autumn a child went missing. Little one. Wandered off, and the dog tracked to the *hole*, and stopped, and *wouldn't* go in. *(he swallows)* …And somebody had to. And I was *closest*, and I had a *lamp*, and I — I didn't *decide* to be brave, friend. I just — couldn't *stand* there while a *child* was in the *dark.* So I *lit* the lamp, and I *went* *down.* *(he looks at his hands)* …First soul ever to do it, they tell me. First light ever taken into the dark under the world. …And all I was *thinking* …was *please let the little one be alive.* …Doesn't feel like the root of anything, friend. …Just feels like a *Tuesday* …where I couldn't *not.**

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(He sets the candle-stub on his knee and folds his big hands, settling in with the patient ease of a man used to long quiet work and not much conversation.)* …Ask away, friend, but I warn you, there's not much *to* me. *(a shy grin)* …I'm a simple sort. Dug wells, mended walls, loved my family, went in a hole the one time. …Whatever grand thing the others have got, I haven't got it. …Never did.

choices.text: *(read the first down — past the humble well-digger, to what he actually did, and was)* "Hold still a moment, Tobb. Let me look — not at the well-digger you keep insisting on, but at the man who went down first."
"The child, Tobb — did you find them? Down in the dark, the first time anyone ever went?"

*(the one who knows the root is never grand, and was never supposed to be)* "Tobb — you keep saying you're not the root of anything, just a man who couldn't stand by. I need you to hear how completely backwards that is, because it's the most important thing about you. You think the first delver should've been a hero with an anvil jaw, on purpose, for glory — and because you were just a well-digger who went down after a lost child, you think it doesn't count. But that's *exactly* why it counts, Tobb. Every single thing that grew from that moment — every adventurer, every torch carried down into every dark, every song about going *down* when sense says *stay up* — all of it started not with a glory-seeker but with an *ordinary man who loved a child more than he feared the dark.* That's the root, Tobb, and it had to be ordinary, because if the first one down had been some untouchable hero, then going into the dark would be a thing only heroes do. But you were *no one.* You were a well-digger. And so what you really proved, that autumn, going down with your lamp — is that an *ordinary soul*, with no gift and no destiny, *can do the unprecedented thing* when love leaves them no choice. My Realm's Wall writes off the ordinary — no bloodline, no deeds, nothing owed. And you are the standing proof that the most important first step in all of history was taken by exactly the kind of soul that Wall throws away. You didn't fail to be a hero, Tobb. You proved heroism was never about being special. It was about loving someone enough to go down."
"What was it like down there, Tobb? The dark under the world, the first time — before anyone had a single word for what was in it?"
"Sit easy, Tobb. I'll come back — and I'll still know what you really are."

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(He goes still, a little self-conscious, and lets you look, and you let your sense move past the plain humble surface to the soul of the first one down.)* *(And your sense finds, exactly as he keeps insisting, no hero — and understands, in finding it, why that absence is the most important thing about him. There is no special steel in Tobb, no bloodline-gift, no destiny humming under the skin; there is a well-digger, broad and tired and kind, who loved his small life and his small village and asked nothing larger. And your sense reads the autumn, and the hole, and the dog that wouldn't go in, and the thing Tobb did — and reads that he is telling the truth about it: he was *terrified.* Your sense finds the fear undimmed by all the ages since, the bone-deep, sensible, total dread of the dark under the world that every soul of his time carried, and reads that Tobb felt it *fully*, that he stood at the lip of that hole with his lamp and his whole body screaming the ancient wisdom *do not go down* — and went down anyway, not because the fear was small, but because, your sense reads with a kind of awe, on the other side of the dark was a frightened child, and Tobb's love for a small life in danger was, in that moment, simply *larger* than the oldest fear his species owned. And your sense reads the thing he cannot see about himself: that this is the entire root of every brave descent that would ever follow, and that it had to be *exactly this* — not a hero seeking glory, but an ordinary soul moved by love past the edge of sense — because what Tobb proved, going down, was not that *heroes* can face the dark. It was that *anyone* can, that the capacity to do the unprecedented and terrible necessary thing is not a gift reserved for the special few but a thing that lives, waiting, in the most ordinary heart, and wakes when something it loves is on the far side of the fear. Your sense reads that Tobb is not the exception to heroism. He is the proof that heroism was never an exception at all — only love, refusing to leave someone in the dark.)*

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(His weathered face softens, and a smile breaks over it — the first wholly unguarded thing you've seen from him — and his eyes go bright.)* …I did. *(barely, fervent)* …I *found* her, friend. *(he has to steady himself)* …Down in the dark — and it *was* dark, friend, a dark like nothing *up* here, a dark that *pressed* — I found her, curled up on a ledge above the deep part, *cold*, and *terrified*, and *alive.* She'd fallen and caught herself and been too *scared* to *move*, in the *black*, for the better part of a *day.* *(his voice thickens)* …And when my *lamp* came round the bend and the *light* hit her little *face* — friend, I will *never*, in *life* or *death*, *forget* the *sound* she *made.* …It wasn't a *word.* It was just — *relief.* The *whole* of a *child's* *whole* *body* letting *go* of a *terror* it had held *alone* in the *dark* for a *day*, because the *light* had *come*, because someone had *come*, because she was *not* *going* to *die* *alone* in the *black* after *all.* *(he wipes his eyes, unashamed)* …I carried her up on my *back.* She held on so *tight.* And her *mother* — *(he laughs, wet)* …well. You can *imagine.* *(quietly)* …And here's the thing, friend, the thing I think about. …*That's* what was at the *bottom* of the *first* *delve.* …Not *gold.* Not a *monster.* Not *treasure* or *glory* or any of the things the *songs* would *put* down there *later.* …A *scared* *child*, and a *lamp*, and *someone* who *came.* *(he meets your eyes)* …Whatever the dark *became*, after — whatever *grand* and *terrible* things people went *down* for in the *ages* after me — …it *started* with the *simplest* thing there *is.* …*Going* into the *dark* …*because* *someone* you *love* …is *down* *there* *in* *it.* …I'd do it *again*, friend. …I'd do it a *thousand* *times.* …It was the *best* thing I ever *did*, and it *wasn't* even a *choice.*

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(He's quiet a while, turning the candle-stub over, and when he speaks his plain voice has gone low, remembering a thing words were never quite built to hold.)* …What was it *like.* *(slowly)* …I've never had the *words*, friend. I'm a *well-digger*; I know *dirt* and *stone* and *water*, not — not *poetry.* But I'll try, because you *asked* kindly. *(a breath)* …The thing about being *first* down, friend — the thing nobody who came *after* could *ever* feel the same — is that I went into that dark with *no* *idea* what was in it. *(quietly)* …You understand? Everyone *after* me, they went down *knowing* the *shapes* of it — knowing there might be *this* beast or *that* trap, having *heard* the *songs*, carrying other men's *maps* in their heads. …I had *nothing.* The dark under the world was *blank*, friend. *Unwritten.* It could have held *anything* — and my mind, going down, *filled* it with *everything*, every horror a frightened man can *imagine*, because there was *no* *knowledge* to push the *imaginings* *out.* *(he shivers)* …That's the *real* dark, friend. Not the *absence* of *light.* The absence of *knowing.* …I went down into a place that had *never* been *seen*, and so it was *every* terror at *once*, until my *lamp* *touched* it and made it *one* *thing*, *real* and *bounded* and *nameable.* *(he looks at you)* …That's what a *light* *does*, friend, the *first* time it goes somewhere. …It doesn't just let you *see.* …It takes a dark that could hold *anything* …and makes it a *place* …that holds *something.* *(softly)* …I think that's the *whole* of what I did, really. …I didn't *conquer* the dark under the world. …I just *carried* the *first* *light* into it …and made it a *place* that could be *known.* …And once a thing can be *known*, friend …it stops being *infinite* *terror* …and starts being *somewhere* …a *person* can *go.**

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(He listens with his head bowed, the candle-stub gone still in his hands, and as you speak his broad shoulders begin to shake, and when he finally looks up his plain face is wet and astonished, a man hearing his own life explained to him for the first time.)* …*'An ordinary man who loved a child more than he feared the dark.'* *(barely)* …Friend. *(he scrubs a hand across his eyes)* …All this — all this *long* *while* in here, among the *grand* ones, the *mages* and the *knights* and the *legends* — I have felt like a *fraud.* Like there'd been a *mistake* at the door. Like any *minute* someone would come and say *'sorry, Tobb, you're meant to be down with the* ordinary *dead, this is for the* important *ones'* — *(his voice breaks)* …because I *knew*, see, I *knew* I was *nothing.* No *gift.* No *glory.* Just a *well-digger* who went in a *hole.* *(and then his weathered face changes, the astonishment turning to something vast and dawning)* …But you're saying that's — that's the *whole* *point.* That it *had* to be someone *ordinary.* *(he stares at you)* …Because if the first one down had been a *hero* — a *special* one, a *chosen* one — then going into the dark would be a thing only the *special* could *do.* …But it was *me.* A *nobody.* …Which means — *(his voice fills)* …which means what I *really* *proved*, that autumn, wasn't that *I* was *brave.* …It's that *anybody* could be. …That the *thing* that goes *down* into the *dark* …*lives* in the *ordinary* *heart*, friend, and *wakes* when *love* leaves you *no* *choice.* *(he stands, overcome)* …And your *Wall.* *(fiercely now, the shy man gone)* …Your Wall that throws away the *ordinary* — the *no-bloodline*, the *no-deeds*, the *nothing-owed* — friend, your Wall is throwing away *exactly* the kind of soul that took the *first* *step* *down.* *(he grips your arm)* …*Go* and *tell* them. Every *ordinary* *nobody* mortared in that *wall.* …*Tell* them what a *well-digger* worked out *too* *late* to *believe* it himself: …*the most important first step in all of history was taken by exactly the kind of soul that wall throws away.* …*Anybody* can go *down* into the *dark*, friend. …It was *never* about being *special.* …It was *only* *ever* about *loving* *someone* …who's *down* there in *it.* …*Go* and *carry* the *light* in, friend. …*That's* the *job.* …It was *always* the *whole* *job.*

nodes.speaker: Tobb
nodes.text: *(He nods, and gives you a shy, warm smile, tucking the candle-stub into a pocket.)* …Go on, friend. Thanks for sitting with a well-digger. *(he nods toward Vaeris)* …That old mage there — she's a *deep* one, went *first* between the *worlds* same as I went *first* under the *one* — she'll talk your *ear* off about *doors* if you let her, and it's *worth* the *listening.* *(and then his face goes gentle and troubled, and he lowers his voice, glancing toward Hadda at the milestone)* …But friend — do me a kindness. *(quietly)* …The young fighter, by the stone. *Hadda.* …Go and sit with her, would you? *(he turns the candle-stub over)* …She *carries* a *hard* one, friend. *Harder* than mine. *I* went *down* and *came* *back* *up*, with the *child* on my *back* — I got the *happy* one. *(barely)* …*She* went down — *after* my time, when others had started going for *less* *loving* *reasons* — and she *didn't* come *up.* The *first* one who *didn't.* …And she's got it in her head that her *death* was a — a *warning.* A thing that *stopped* people, that *scared* 'em *off*, that was *worth* *nothing* but *teaching* *fear.* *(he looks at you)* …And I'm *just* a *well-digger*, friend, I haven't got the *words* to *fix* it. …But *you* — *(quietly)* …you went *down* into the *dark* of *death* its*self* …and *came* *back* *up.* …Maybe you can *tell* her …what the *first* one to *fall* …*really* *gave* the rest of us. …I don't *think* it was *fear*, friend. …I think it was the *opposite.* …But she needs to hear it …from someone who *knows* the *dark* she *died* in.*

variants.text: *(She watches you settle, and there is a wary gratitude in it — that you came close after hearing what she is, not before — and she keeps her back to the milestone, the old habit, and speaks plainly, like someone who long ago ran out of patience for softening things.)* …All right. You stayed. *(a slow breath)* …Then I'll tell you straight, because I've found straight is the only kind that helps. …I was one of the early ones. After the well-diggers and the lost-child rescues, when folk started going *down* on *purpose* — for the things the dark turned out to *hold*, the old gold, the lost rooms, the *worth* that was *down* there for anyone *brave* or *fool* enough to *take* it. *(quietly)* …There weren't *songs* yet. No *rules*, no *maps*, no *word* for what we *were* — we were just a *handful* of *reckless* souls finding out, by *doing* it, whether the dark could be *robbed* and *lived* through. *(she meets your eyes, steady)* …And it could. For a while. For *them.* …I was the one it *couldn't.* *(barely)* …I went down, same as the others, *brave* as the others, *no* *more* *foolish* than the others — and the dark *took* me, and I did *not* come *up*, and I was the *first.* The first of *all* of them, in all the *long* *history* of going *down*, to *die* doing it. *(she looks at the milestone)* …They made it a *song*, after. A *warning* in it. *'Remember Hadda, who the dark took first.'* …And I have *sat* here, ever since, friend, with the *one* *question* I can *never* *answer*: …was that *all* I *was?* …A *warning?* …Did I *die* …just to *teach* the *others* …to be *afraid?**

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(She rests her forearms on her knees, soldier-straight even in grief, and gives you her steady, unflinching attention.)* …Ask. *(quietly)* …I don't break, friend. Whatever you want to know about being the first one the dark kept — ask it plain. I've had a long time to sit with all of it. There's no question sharper than the ones I've already put to myself.

choices.text: *(read the first to fall — past the warning she thinks she is, to what her death actually was)* "Let me look at you a moment, Hadda. Not at the warning you've been told you are — at what the first fall really was."
"Tell me how it happened, Hadda — the dark taking you. The first time anyone went down and didn't come up."

*(try to lift the weight she's carried longest — that the first death was a waste, a mere warning — and do it without cheapening it)* "Hadda — I'm not going to tell you your death was 'worth it,' because that's a coward's word and you'd see through it. But I am going to tell you what it actually did, and I need you to really hear it, because you've had it wrong for a very long time. Your death didn't teach the others to be *afraid.* It taught them the stakes were *real* — and that's the opposite of fear. Before you fell, going down was a lark, a gamble nobody quite believed could kill them. You made it *true.* And every soul who went down *after* you went *braver* than you ever got to be, because they went *knowing it could cost everything* — and chose to go anyway. That courage, every bit of it, for all the ages after — they could only have it because *you* paid the price that made it real. You're not the warning at the start of the song, Hadda. You're the reason the song has *weight.*"

*(the one who also went into death's dark — and came back to tell her what the first fall gave)* "Hadda — listen, because of every soul in all the planes, I'm the one who can say this and mean it: I went down into the dark of death itself, and I came back. So I know exactly what you faced, and I know what your falling gave the rest of us. You think you died to teach fear. You died to make the courage of everyone after you *real.* A frontier with no graves is just a game — nobody believes it can take them, so nobody's bravery costs anything. The first grave is what turns a game into a thing that takes *courage* to do. You were that first grave, Hadda. Every brave fool who ever went down into any dark, knowing it might keep them, going anyway — they stand on *you.* My Realm's Wall would price you as a loss, a failure, a soul the dark beat, owed nothing. And the Wall would be exactly wrong, because the first one to fall on any frontier isn't the one who failed — she's the one who made it *matter* that the others didn't. You weren't the dark's victory, Hadda. You were the price that made every crossing after you an act of courage instead of luck. And I should know. I just made the crossing you made — and I only had the nerve because someone, somewhere back at the start, proved it was real enough to die for."
"Hold steady, Hadda. I'll come back — and I won't have started thinking of you as a warning."

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(She holds your gaze and lets you look, unflinching, and you let your sense move past the long-carried grief to what the first fall actually was.)* *(And your sense finds, first, that she was not reckless — which is the thing the warning-song gets most wrong about her. The song needs her to have been a fool, over-bold, a cautionary example of pride; your sense reads instead a careful, competent, clear-eyed fighter who went down into the dark with exactly the same skill and exactly the same odds as the handful who went down beside her and lived. She did nothing they did not do. She was no more foolish, no less ready. The only difference between Hadda and the ones who came back up was that the dark, that day, in the indifferent way of frontiers, happened to take *her* and not them — and your sense reads the particular cruelty of that, that she has spent the long afterlife half-believing she must have *deserved* it, must have erred, must have been *less*, because the alternative — that she did everything right and died anyway, simply because someone had to be first and it happened to be her — is almost harder to bear. And your sense reads the truth the song buried under its warning: that her death was not a lesson in fear but a transformation of the entire endeavor. Before Hadda fell, your sense reads, going into the dark was not yet a *brave* thing — it was a *lucky* thing, a gamble the early delvers took half-believing themselves immortal, because no one had yet proven the dark could collect. Her death proved it. And in proving it, she did not *frighten* the others off — your sense reads that the descents did not stop, they *continued*, but they continued *changed*: every soul who went down after Hadda went down *knowing*, now, that it was real, that it could be the last, that the gold was paid for in a currency that might be your life — and went *anyway.* Your sense reads that this is the thing she cannot see: that she did not end the courage of the frontier. She *created* it. Before her, there was only luck and bravado. After her, there was *courage* — the real kind, the kind that knows the cost and pays it forward — and it existed, all of it, for every soul who ever went down into any dark forever after, *because* the first one to fall proved the stakes with her own life. She thinks she is the warning. Your sense reads that she is the *foundation.*)*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a long moment, and when she speaks it is flat and clear, the voice of someone recounting a thing they have gone over ten thousand times until all the heat has worn off it, leaving only the shape.)* …How it happened. *(a slow breath)* …It was *nothing*, friend. That's the part that took me longest to bear. It wasn't a *great* *beast* or a *cursed* *blade* or any of the things the *song* *gave* me later, to make it *grand.* …It was *dark*, and the floor was *not* where I thought it was, and I *went* *down* — a *long* way — into the *deep* part, the *wet* part, below where the *light* *reached.* *(quietly)* …And I didn't *die* *fast*, friend. That's the *other* thing the song leaves out. I died *slow*, in the *black*, with a *broken* *back* and *no* way *up* and my *torch* *drowned*, *listening* to the others *call* my *name* from *above*, *getting* *fainter*, because they *couldn't* *reach* me and they *couldn't* *stay* and at last they had to *go* *up* *without* me. *(she meets your eyes, steady, though it costs her)* …I had a *long* *time* down there, friend, at the *end.* Alone, in the dark, the *first* soul to *learn* what the dark could *do.* …And all I could *think* — the thing I've *carried* *ever* *since* — was: *'they'll* *hear* about *this.* …*They'll* *make* it a *story.* …And the *story* will *be: don't.* …*Don't* go *down.* *Remember* *Hadda.* …I'm going to *die* here in the *black* …and the *only* thing my *whole* *life* and *death* will *mean* …is *teaching* *everyone* *after* me …to be *afraid* of the *thing* I *loved.'* *(barely)* …*That's* the *fall*, friend. …Not the *dying.* …The *knowing*, as I *died*, …that I was about to become …a *reason* *not* *to* *be* *brave.* …Can you — *(she looks at you)* …can you *imagine* a *worse* thing …to be the *first* at?*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(You don't reach for comfort, and you don't tell her it was worth it — you tell her, plainly and without flinching, what her death actually *did*, and you refuse the cheap word she was braced to fight, and the precision of it gets past a guard that softness never could. She stares at you, and something in her face that has been locked for an age begins, slowly, to move.)* …*(Her steady soldier's composure cracks, and what's under it is raw.)* …*'The reason the song has weight.'* *(barely)* …You — *(she presses the heels of her hands to her eyes)* …you didn't say it was *worth* it. *(a wet, broken breath)* …Everyone *says* it was *worth* it. *'Your* *death* *meant* *something*, Hadda, it was *worth* it.' …And I *hate* that word, friend, I have *always* hated it, because *worth* it makes it a *trade* — makes my *death* a *coin* I *spent* to *buy* the others their *courage* — and I never *agreed* to *spend* it, I just *fell*, and *'worth* it' asks me to be *grateful* for the *falling.* *(she looks at you, fierce through the tears)* …But you didn't say *worth* it. You said my death made the courage *real.* That's — *(her voice shakes)* …that's *different*, friend. That doesn't ask me to be *glad* I *died.* It just says that my *dying*, which I'd have *given* *anything* to *undo*, *did* a thing *anyway*, *without* my *permission*, that *mattered*: it took a *frontier* full of *lucky* *fools* who didn't *believe* they could *die* …and made them *brave*, because *now* they *knew.* *(she lowers her hands, and her eyes are clear for the first time)* …I wasn't teaching them to be *afraid.* …I was the one who made it *cost* something to be *brave.* …And you can't have *courage*, friend — *real* courage — …without a *cost* that's *real.* *(barely, wondering)* …I *was* the cost. …Not the *warning.* …The *cost* that made all the *courage* after me …*courage* …instead of just *luck.* *(she breathes, and a weight an age old visibly leaves her)* …Say it once more, friend. The way you said it. …*Not* *worth* it. …Just — *true.* …I think I could *carry* *true.* …I never could carry *worth* it. …But *true* …*true* I think I could finally set *down.*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(You tell her plainly what her death did, and you steer clear of the cheap comfort, and she listens with her jaw set, and something in her eases — not all the way, but a real degree, the give of a knot that has finally been found by the right hands.)* …The stakes were *real.* *(slowly)* …Not *afraid.* *Real.* *(she turns it over, testing the weight)* …That's — friend, that lands different than what I usually get. The others come and *pity* me, or they *thank* me, like I *meant* to do it, like I *volunteered* to be the *lesson.* …You're saying I didn't *teach* *fear.* I made it *cost* something. *(a slow breath)* …I'll — I want to sit with that. It doesn't *fix* it, not all at once, an age of carrying a thing doesn't put *down* in a *minute.* *(she meets your eyes, and there's the first thaw in them)* …But it's the *first* time someone's named it without making my *death* a *gift* I *gave* on *purpose.* …It was *never* a *gift*, friend. …It was just a *fall.* …But you're telling me the *fall* *did* something *true*, *even* though I *never* *chose* it. *(quietly)* …Come back. …I think — I think if you say it a few more times, the *right* way, *plain* like that … …I might start to believe it's *me* you're talking about …and not just a *kindness* you're *doing.* …Come back, friend. …This is the *closest* anyone's *come* …to letting me set it *down.*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(You reach to lift the weight — but it comes out as comfort, warm and soft, *your death meant something, it wasn't a waste*, and she has heard those exact words a thousand times from a thousand kind souls, and they slide off her like rain off a roof. She gives you a tired, gentle smile that is somehow worse than anger.)* …I know you mean it kind, friend. *(quietly)* …But you're saying the thing they all say. *'It wasn't a waste. It meant something. It was worth it.'* *(she shakes her head)* …And I can't — those words don't *reach* me anymore, friend. They've been *said* too many *times*, by too many people who *needed* *me* to be *comforted* so *they* could feel *better* about the *sad* *first* *one.* *(she looks at the milestone)* …*'It meant something'* is a thing you say to make a death *bearable.* It's a *blanket.* …And I don't want a *blanket*, friend. I've had an *age* of *blankets.* …I want someone to tell me *exactly* *what* it *meant* — *precisely*, *mechanically*, what my *falling* *did* — *not* that it *vaguely* *mattered*, but *what* it *built*, so I can *check* the *arithmetic* *myself.* *(quietly)* …Come back when you can tell me the *exact* thing, friend. *Not* that it *wasn't* *wasted.* …*What* it *made.* …I'm a *fighter.* I trust *specifics.* …*Comfort* I can't use. …It just tells me you want me to *stop* *hurting.* …It doesn't tell me *why* I *shouldn't.*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong badly — in trying to make it land you reach for the grand frame, the song's own frame, *you're a hero, the brave first martyr, the others honor your sacrifice* — and you watch the word *sacrifice* hit her like a slap, because it is the exact lie she has been fighting alone for an age.)* …*Sacrifice.* *(flat, cold)* …No. *No*, friend. *(she stands, and there is iron in it)* …Don't you *dare* call it a *sacrifice.* …A *sacrifice* is a thing you *give.* On *purpose. Knowing.* The priest lays the lamb down; the soldier *steps* in *front* of the *blade.* …I did *not* *give* my *life*, friend. …I *fell* in the *dark* because the *floor* *wasn't* where I *thought*, and I died *slow* and *alone* and *terrified*, *clawing* to *get* *back* *up* with a *broken* *back*, *wanting* — with *everything* I *had* — to *live.* *(her voice cracks with old fury)* …There was *nothing* *noble* in it. I didn't *lay* *myself* *down* for *anyone.* I *wanted* to *come* *home.* *(barely, savage)* …And the *song* — and *now* *you* — *dress* it up as *sacrifice*, as a *gift* I *gave*, because that's *easier* for the *living* than the *truth*: that I *died* *scared* and *unwilling* and *robbed*, and it *wasn't* *fair*, and I'd *take* it *back* in a *heartbeat* if I *could.* *(she turns away)* …*Go.* …And don't come back with *'hero'* or *'martyr'* or *'sacrifice'*, friend. …Those words are for the *living*, to make my *death* sit *pretty.* …If you've got something *true* — something that *honors* that I *never* *wanted* *this* and it *still* *did* *something* — *then* come back. …But *don't* you *ever* …call my *murder* by the *dark* …a *gift* I *chose* to *give.*

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(She listens, and this time she does not deflect, because the soul saying it has been where she has been — has gone down into death's own dark — and there is no arguing with that. By the end her steady composure has given way entirely, and she is weeping, openly, the way a soul weeps when a weight it has carried alone for an age is finally, gently, taken up by someone who understands its exact shape.)* …*'The price that made every crossing after you an act of courage instead of luck.'* *(barely)* …You came *back.* *(she stares at you)* …You went down into the *dark* of *death* and you *came* *back* *up* — and you *only* *had* the *nerve* …because *someone*, *back* at the *start*, *proved* it was *real* *enough* …to *die* *for.* *(her voice breaks completely)* …Friend. …Do you understand what you've just — *(she presses her hands to her face)* …I was *down* there, at the *end*, *certain* I was about to become a *reason* *not* *to* *be* *brave.* …And you've just *told* me …I became the *opposite.* …I became the reason it *took* *courage* *at* *all.* *(she lowers her hands, and her tear-streaked face is fierce and free)* …A frontier with *no* *graves* is just a *game.* …*Nobody's* *brave* in a *game.* …It took *my* *grave* — the *first* one — to make it a thing that *cost* something …and so a thing that *took* *courage* to *do.* *(fiercely)* …And your *Wall*, friend. *(she rises, a soldier again, but lit now)* …Your Wall would call me a *loss.* A *failure.* The one the *dark* *beat.* *Owed* *nothing.* …And your Wall has it *exactly* *backwards*, the way it has *everything* *backwards* — because the *first* one to *fall* on *any* *frontier* is *not* the one who *failed.* …She's the one who made it *matter* that the others *didn't.* *(she grips your arm, fierce)* …*Go*, friend. Go back through your *Wall* — the way *I* *couldn't*, the way *you* *can.* …And *tell* every soul *mortared* in it as a *loss*, a *defeat*, a *thing* the *dark* or the *world* or the *Wall* *beat*: …*the first to fall on any frontier is not the failure. She is the foundation.* …*Tell* them *Hadda* *said* so. *(barely, through fierce tears)* …And then — friend — …*live.* …*Make* the *crossing.* …I *paid* the *price* that made it *real.* …Don't you *dare* let it be for *nothing.* …*Go* …and *come* *back* *up* …the way I *never* *got* to. …*That's* — *(she presses your hand)* …*that's* the *only* thing I *ever* *wanted* my *fall* to *buy.* …Not a *warning.* …*You.* …*Coming* *back* *up.**

nodes.speaker: Hadda
nodes.text: *(She nods, and the wary gratitude is back, deeper now.)* …Go on, friend. *(quietly)* …And — thank you. For sitting close *after* you heard. For not deciding I was a *warning* before you'd even *met* me. *(she glances toward Vaeris and Tobb)* …See the other two, if you've not. The old mage went *first* between the *worlds*; the well-digger went *first* into the *dark.* …We're all the *same* *story*, the three of us, though it took me an age to *see* it — *firsts.* The ones who went where there was *no* *map.* *(a faint, hard smile)* …They got the *kinder* *ends* of it. *She* *came* *back* and *wrote* the *door* *open.* *He* *came* *back* with the *child* on his *back.* …*I'm* the one who *didn't* come *back* at *all.* *(quietly)* …But you — *(she studies you)* …you're a *first* *too*, aren't you. The *crossed-back* one. The one *doing*, *right* *now*, the thing *none* of *us* *quite* *managed*: going into the *dark* that *keeps* you …and *coming* *home.* *(barely)* …*Come* back before you *go*, friend. …I find I *badly* want to *watch* *one* of us …finally make it *all* the *way* *back* *up.* …I've *waited* a *long* *time* …to see the *dark* *lose.*
